article_meta.title: Business Climate change study that overestimated economic impact is pulled — but not before banks relied on it By Taylor Herzlich Published Dec

transcript.content:
Classify this article's political lean as: "Left", "Lean Left", "Center", "Lean Right", or "Right". Respond ONLY with valid JSON — no prose, no markdown fences:
{"lean":"...","confidence":0.0,"reasoning":"...","key_indicators":["..."]}

---
HEADLINE: Business Climate change study that overestimated economic impact is pulled — but not before banks relied on it By Taylor Herzlich Published Dec
SOURCE: New York Post (News)

ARTICLE:

Business Climate change study that overestimated economic impact is pulled — but not before banks relied on it By Taylor Herzlich Published Dec. 3, 2025, 4:49 p.m. ET The scientific journal Nature retracted an influential paper that overestimated the economic toll of climate change – but not until after central banks around the world had used it to create risk management scenarios. The three scientists who worked on the study on Wednesday cited "substantial" issues with the paper, which was originally published in Nature in April 2024. The article estimated a massive 62% drop in worldwide economic output by 2100 if carbon emissions continue unabated. A car drives through a flooded street in Rosemead, California, on Oct. 14. AFP via Getty Images But earlier this year, a Nature article by a separate team of economists noted that the climate study's findings were largely skewed by problems with the data for just one country, Uzbekistan. If numbers for the Central Asian nation were excluded from the data set, instead of a 62% decline in economic output, there would be a 23% drop – still a big blow, but not nearly as catastrophic as initially suggested. Explore More Fed slashes interest rates by quarter point for third time in a row Instacart uses shady algorithm to charge different prices to individual customers — in the same stores: bombshell study Pepsi axing snacks, sodas from its lineup— and slashing prices in cost-cutting marathon The faulty number – which was roughly three times typical estimates – quickly made headlines and was cited by policymakers around the world, including the World Bank and the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development. It was also used by the Network for Greening the Financial System last year as it updated its scenarios modeling the expected economic impact of climate change. Charlie Gasparino has his finger on the pulse of where business, politics and finance meet Sign up to receive On The Money by Charlie Gasparino in your inbox every Thursday. Thanks for signing up! Enter your email address Please provide a valid email. By clicking above you agree to the Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. Never miss a story. Check out more newsletters NGFS is a global network of central banks and financial supervisors with more than 150 members across nearly 90 countries, including the People's Bank of China, the European Central Bank, the Bank of England – and, until earlier this year, the Federal Reserve. "The NGFS welcomes the academic debate and looks forward to new contributions toward a better understanding of the micro and macroeconomic impact of climate change," the organization said in a Wednesday statement. "The NGFS will keep monitoring developments in this critical area of research and an updated methodology will be used for the next iteration of long-term scenarios, to be published end of 2026." Damage from rampant flooding at an RV park in Kerrville, Texas. LP Media Over the summer, the climate study's authors – Maximilian Kotz, Anders Levermann and Leonie Wenz of the Potsdam Institute in Germany – reviewed and amended the paper in light of the critical findings. But they later acknowledged that their errors were "too substantial for a correction," leading to the retraction this week. The authors plan to revise and resubmit the paper, according to Nature. Kotz, the lead author of the paper, told Bloomberg there's still data pointing to "very substantial impacts of climate change on the economy," which justifies a "quite ambitious climate policy." It's "important to remember what the broader field looks like already," and that other studies are not arriving at "wildly different conclusions," he said. Wenz added that "there are more and more studies using different data and different methodological approaches that actually find pretty high economic damages and a very strong case for climate change mitigation."
```json
{
  "lean": "Right",
  "confidence": 0.82,
  "reasoning": "This article exhibits several characteristics typical of right-leaning climate change coverage. The framing emphasizes the retraction and error in climate science rather than the broader scientific consensus on climate impacts. The headline and narrative structure highlight that 'banks relied on' flawed data, suggesting regulatory overreach or premature policy action based on uncertain science. The article gives prominent placement to the error (62% vs 23% decline) while downplaying that a 23% economic decline is still severe. The inclusion of scientists' defenses of climate policy is relegated to the end and framed as damage control. The mention that the Federal Reserve left NGFS 'earlier this year' (without context) subtly validates skepticism of climate-focused financial regulation. The source (New York Post) is a right-leaning publication, and the framing aligns with conservative skepticism toward climate policy and regulatory frameworks. While factually reporting a genuine retraction, the editorial choices in emphasis and structure serve a narrative skeptical of climate change economics and related policy interventions.",
  "key_indicators": [
    "Emphasis on scientific error and retraction over broader climate consensus",
    "Framing suggests premature policy action based on flawed data",
    "Highlighting that banks and central banks 'relied on' the study implies regulatory overreach",
    "Downplaying the still-significant revised estimates (23% decline)",
    "Scientists' continued defense of climate policy relegated to end of article",
    "Subtle validation of Fed leaving climate banking network",
    "Source is New York Post, a right-leaning publication",
    "Overall narrative structure serves climate policy skepticism"
  ]
}
```

parsed_output.reasoning: This article exhibits several characteristics typical of right-leaning climate change coverage. The framing emphasizes the retraction and error in climate science rather than the broader scientific consensus on climate impacts. The headline and narrative structure highlight that 'banks relied on' flawed data, suggesting regulatory overreach or premature policy action based on uncertain science. The article gives prominent placement to the error (62% vs 23% decline) while downplaying that a 23% economic decline is still severe. The inclusion of scientists' defenses of climate policy is relegated to the end and framed as damage control. The mention that the Federal Reserve left NGFS 'earlier this year' (without context) subtly validates skepticism of climate-focused financial regulation. The source (New York Post) is a right-leaning publication, and the framing aligns with conservative skepticism toward climate policy and regulatory frameworks. While factually reporting a genuine retraction, the editorial choices in emphasis and structure serve a narrative skeptical of climate change economics and related policy interventions.
